subtopic_detail: Long-term lithium monitoring frequency in stable bipolar I disorder
stem: A 38-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on lithium 1000 mg nightly for 5 years with no acute mood episodes. Her most recent 12-hour trough lithium level is 0.7 mmol/L. She is otherwise well and on no other regular medications. She has no symptoms of polyuria or polydipsia. Her primary care GP is reviewing her chronic disease management plan and asks about monitoring intervals. She has had no surgery or significant illness in the past year.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic, no fine tremor, no weight gain over 12 months
data_table.Investigations: Most recent (3 months ago): lithium 0.7 mmol/L, TSH 2.4 mIU/L, eGFR 92, calcium corrected 2.34 mmol/L; ECG normal

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate monitoring plan?

options.text: Six-monthly lithium level, TSH, eGFR and calcium, with a 12-hourly trough lithium level taken 5 to 7 days after any dose change or new interacting medication
options.rationale: AU practice on stable lithium recommends 6-monthly serum lithium, renal, thyroid and calcium monitoring with trough levels 12 hours post dose, and additional levels around dose or interacting-medication changes. (Source: RANZCP clinical practice guidelines on mood disorders; AMH lithium monograph)

options.text: Annual TSH and lithium level only, with no renal or calcium monitoring required and no additional monitoring around dose or interacting-medication changes
options.rationale: Lithium causes nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, chronic kidney injury and hyperparathyroidism; renal and calcium monitoring is mandatory. (Source: RANZCP mood disorders guideline)

options.text: Five-yearly lithium level only with no other monitoring intervals, on the basis that her current ranges have been stable for 5 consecutive years at therapeutic targets without symptoms
options.rationale: Lithium toxicity, renal injury and thyroid disease can develop silently; long intervals miss reversible harms. (Source: RANZCP mood disorders guideline)

options.text: Replace lithium with sodium valproate empirically without further monitoring of any chemistry, levels or organ function and discontinue all bipolar-specific reviews going forward
options.rationale: Stable disease on lithium is a reason to continue lithium with monitoring, not to switch agents. (Source: RANZCP mood disorders guideline)

options.text: Monthly lithium level and ECG indefinitely as the default monitoring schedule for any patient on lithium, regardless of stability or duration of therapy
options.rationale: Monthly testing is reserved for initiation, dose change or instability; routine stable monitoring is at 6 to 12 monthly intervals. (Source: RANZCP mood disorders guideline)

explanation.summary: Long-term lithium monitoring in a stable patient is at 6-monthly intervals and covers a 12-hour trough lithium level (target 0.4 to 1.0 mmol/L for maintenance), eGFR, TSH and calcium. Additional levels are drawn around dose changes, new interacting medications (NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, thiazides), and intercurrent illness. Toxicity risk rises with dehydration, infection and renal injury. Annual cardiometabolic review and review of contraception remain relevant.
key_points: Stable lithium: 6-monthly lithium, eGFR, TSH and calcium
Trough lithium 12 hours post dose; therapeutic 0.4 to 1.0 mmol/L for maintenance
Monitor more frequently around dose changes, new interacting drugs and intercurrent illness
explanation.pearls: Maintenance lithium trough 0.4 to 1.0 mmol/L drawn 12 hours post dose with 6-monthly eGFR, TSH and calcium balances relapse prevention with the risks of nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, chronic kidney injury and hyperparathyroidism.

subtopic_detail: First-line pharmacotherapy for tobacco use disorder in an adult smoker
stem: A 47-year-old man smokes 20 cigarettes a day, has done so for 25 years, and wants to quit. He has tried nicotine patches without behavioural support twice in the past with relapse within weeks. He has no history of seizures, no eating disorder, no recent myocardial infarction in the last month and no severe renal impairment. He has a remote episode of mild depression 8 years ago, fully resolved without treatment. He is on no regular medications.

data_table.Examination: No respiratory distress, breath sounds normal; mild dental staining
data_table.Investigations: FEV1/FVC 0.74; eGFR 88; HbA1c 5.5%; PHQ-9 score 3 (minimal symptoms)

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Encourage transition to electronic cigarettes (vapes) without any other pharmacological cessation aid as the recommended first-line strategy in this patient with prior NRT failures
options.rationale: RACGP positions vaping as a second-line cessation aid, considered only when first-line approved pharmacotherapies fail; not first-line. (Source: RACGP supporting smoking cessation)

options.text: Varenicline 0.5 mg daily for 3 days, then 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days, then 1 mg twice daily for 12 weeks, with a quit date set for day 8, alongside behavioural support
options.rationale: Varenicline plus behavioural support is the AU first-line pharmacotherapy for tobacco use disorder, with the licensed titration to 1 mg twice daily for 12 weeks. (Source: RACGP supporting smoking cessation; AMH varenicline)

options.text: Brief nicotine replacement therapy for 3 days only at low dose to minimise systemic exposure, with no concurrent behavioural support, no extended duration, and no longer-acting agent considered
options.rationale: Standard NRT is 8 to 12 weeks at full dose combining a patch with short-acting formulation; 3 days is sub-therapeutic. (Source: RACGP supporting smoking cessation)

options.text: Bupropion sustained-release 150 mg daily as monotherapy in a patient with an active eating disorder requiring close psychiatric follow-up and concurrent low-dose benzodiazepine support
options.rationale: Bupropion is contraindicated in active eating disorders and is not first-line here; behavioural support is also needed. (Source: RACGP supporting smoking cessation)

options.text: Oral naltrexone 50 mg daily indefinitely as monotherapy specifically targeting nicotine craving, with no other cessation aid or behavioural support added at this initial visit
options.rationale: Naltrexone is not an approved nicotine cessation pharmacotherapy in Australia. (Source: RACGP supporting smoking cessation)

explanation.summary: First-line AU pharmacotherapy for tobacco use disorder is varenicline (12 weeks at 1 mg twice daily after titration), combination NRT (patch plus short-acting formulation for 8 to 12 weeks), or bupropion sustained-release. Behavioural support roughly doubles quit rates compared with pharmacotherapy alone. Quitline and brief intervention models support adherence. Vaping is a second-line aid considered when first-line pharmacotherapies fail. Bupropion is contraindicated in active eating disorders and seizure disorders.
key_points: Varenicline 12 weeks with behavioural support is AU first-line
Combination NRT (patch plus short-acting) doubles quit rates over patch alone
Bupropion is contraindicated in active eating disorders or seizure history
explanation.pearls: Varenicline 1 mg twice daily for 12 weeks (titrated over the first week) achieves around 25 percent abstinence at 6 months, roughly double the rate of NRT patch monotherapy.

subtopic_detail: Adult ADHD assessment and stimulant initiation pathway
stem: A 27-year-old graphic designer reports lifelong inattention, frequent task-switching and procrastination that now impair his work. He recalls academic difficulties from primary school. He drinks alcohol moderately and uses no other substances. He has no cardiac history, no significant mood symptoms today and no anxiety disorder requiring treatment. He has no first-degree relative with sudden cardiac death. His GP is the first doctor to assess these concerns formally.

data_table.Examination: Mental state: euthymic, no thought disorder, concentration intermittently poor on serial 7s; PHQ-9 score 4, GAD-7 score 5
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus rhythm, normal QTc; AUDIT score 5; collateral history from mother confirms childhood inattention at school

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Commence a trial of oral methylphenidate immediate-release 10 mg twice daily empirically at this initial visit without further diagnostic workup, collateral history or specialist referral
options.rationale: Australian regulations require psychiatrist or paediatrician initiation of stimulants for ADHD; GPs cannot initiate. (Source: RACGP ADHD position; Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)

options.text: Commence an SSRI as first-line pharmacotherapy for adult ADHD symptoms with no further specialist assessment, no further investigation and no plan to consider stimulants for this presentation
options.rationale: SSRIs are not first-line for ADHD; they may treat comorbid depression or anxiety but do not address core ADHD symptoms. (Source: AADPA ADHD clinical guideline)

options.text: Complete a structured DSM-5 ADHD interview, gather corroborating childhood history, screen for comorbid mood, anxiety and substance use, then refer to a psychiatrist for stimulant initiation under AU prescribing restrictions
options.rationale: Adult ADHD diagnosis requires DSM-5 symptoms in childhood and adulthood with corroborating history; comorbidity screening guides management. Stimulant initiation must be by a psychiatrist (or paediatrician) under state authority and PBS rules. (Source: AADPA ADHD clinical guideline)

options.text: Refer for CBT alone without any consideration of stimulant or non-stimulant pharmacotherapy, regardless of severity, functional impairment or response to non-pharmacological interventions over time
options.rationale: CBT is adjunctive in moderate to severe ADHD; pharmacotherapy remains the most effective core treatment. (Source: AADPA ADHD clinical guideline)

options.text: Trial of lithium for impulse control as the first pharmacological intervention with no specialist referral, no structured ADHD diagnostic interview, and no consideration of stimulant or non-stimulant agents
options.rationale: Lithium has no role in primary ADHD pharmacotherapy. (Source: AADPA ADHD clinical guideline)

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD diagnosis requires DSM-5 symptoms beginning before age 12, persisting into adulthood with functional impairment across more than one setting, after exclusion of competing diagnoses. Corroborative childhood history (parent, school reports) strengthens diagnostic confidence. Comorbidity with mood, anxiety, substance use and learning disorders is the rule rather than the exception. Treatment is multimodal: psychoeducation, environmental modification, and pharmacotherapy (stimulants first-line in non-substance-use comorbidity, atomoxetine for substance use risk). Stimulant initiation is restricted to psychiatrists and paediatricians under Australian state authorities.
key_points: Adult ADHD diagnosis requires DSM-5 symptoms from childhood persisting into adulthood
Corroborative childhood history strengthens the diagnosis
Stimulants are initiated by a psychiatrist or paediatrician under AU state authorities
explanation.pearls: The AADPA 2022 guideline anchors adult ADHD diagnosis in structured DSM-5 interviewing with corroborative childhood history; GP-initiated stimulants are not legal in any Australian state.

subtopic_detail: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose with QRS prolongation and hypotension
stem: A 24-year-old woman is brought to ED 2 hours after ingestion of an estimated 4 grams of amitriptyline. She is now drowsy, hypotensive and tachycardic. She had a single seizure in the ambulance. The ECG demonstrates sinus tachycardia with a QRS duration of 140 ms and a right axis. She has no significant past medical history, no allergies and is not pregnant. There is no concurrent alcohol or other drug ingestion documented.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 86/52, HR 124, RR 22, SpO2 96% on room air, Temp 36.8 degrees Celsius, GCS 11
data_table.Examination: Dilated pupils 6 mm, dry mucous membranes, urinary retention; ECG QRS 140 ms, right axis
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.30, lactate 3.6 mmol/L, K 3.6 mmol/L, Na 138 mmol/L; salicylate and paracetamol levels negative; serum amitriptyline pending

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Intravenous lipid emulsion 20 percent at 1.5 mL/kg as a sole agent with no concurrent bicarbonate, no airway support and no antiarrhythmic therapy in this presentation
options.rationale: Lipid emulsion is a salvage option for refractory toxicity; it is not the first-line for QRS widening. (Source: NSW Health Poisons Information Centre TCA monograph; Murray Toxicology 4e)
source_refs: NSW Poisons Information Centre TCA guideline

options.text: Single dose activated charcoal 50 g orally 4 hours after the ingestion as the sole and primary intervention, with no other haemodynamic or cardiac stabilising therapy commenced at this time
options.rationale: Activated charcoal at 4 hours after an ingestion of a slow-absorbed drug is of marginal benefit and does not address the cardiotoxicity. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre TCA guideline)
source_refs: NSW Poisons Information Centre TCA guideline

options.text: Intravenous diazepam 10 mg as a single dose for the seizure with no further cardiovascular intervention beyond observation in a monitored bay over the next 4 hours
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines treat seizures but do not address the sodium channel blockade driving QRS widening and hypotension. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre TCA guideline)
source_refs: NSW Poisons Information Centre TCA guideline

options.text: Intravenous 8.4 percent sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg as a bolus to narrow the QRS and target arterial pH 7.45 to 7.55, with airway support and crystalloid resuscitation as adjuncts
options.rationale: TCA cardiotoxicity from fast sodium channel blockade is reversed with hypertonic sodium bicarbonate aiming for QRS narrowing and arterial pH 7.45 to 7.55; airway and circulation support continue alongside. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre TCA guideline)
source_refs: NSW Poisons Information Centre TCA guideline

options.text: Initiation of urgent haemodialysis as the sole and primary management strategy for TCA cardiotoxicity, with no concurrent bicarbonate therapy and no specific airway or cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: TCAs are highly protein-bound with a large volume of distribution; haemodialysis is not effective. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre TCA guideline)
source_refs: NSW Poisons Information Centre TCA guideline

explanation.summary: TCA overdose produces sodium channel blockade with QRS prolongation, ventricular arrhythmia, seizures, anticholinergic and alpha-blocker effects. Hypertonic sodium bicarbonate (1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus, repeated as needed) reverses sodium channel blockade by both pH alkalinisation and sodium loading, targeting QRS under 100 ms and arterial pH 7.45 to 7.55. Adjuncts: airway protection, crystalloid plus noradrenaline for hypotension, benzodiazepines for seizures, lipid emulsion in refractory shock. Haemodialysis is ineffective due to high protein binding and large volume of distribution.
key_points: QRS over 100 ms in TCA overdose indicates sodium channel blockade
Hypertonic sodium bicarbonate is the first-line antidote
Target arterial pH 7.45 to 7.55 and QRS narrowing
explanation.pearls: QRS over 100 ms in suspected TCA overdose predicts seizures and arrhythmia and is the trigger for sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg IV bolus; an R wave over 3 mm in aVR is corroborative.

sources.label: NSW Poisons Information Centre TCA guideline

subtopic_detail: Catatonia in major depression with positive lorazepam challenge
stem: A 58-year-old woman with a history of recurrent major depressive disorder is admitted after 6 days of becoming progressively mute, immobile and refusing food. She maintains odd postures and exhibits waxy flexibility. There is no fever. Investigations exclude infection, hypoglycaemia, electrolyte disturbance and intoxication. CT brain is unremarkable. She has been off her usual sertraline for 3 weeks before admission. There is no history of antipsychotic exposure.

data_table.Examination: Mutism, immobility, posturing, waxy flexibility; no rigidity, no tremor, no autonomic instability; Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale 14
data_table.Investigations: Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.0, glucose 5.4 mmol/L, CK 220 U/L, CRP 4 mg/L, ammonia 28 micromol/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L; CT brain unremarkable

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial step?

options.text: Commence electroconvulsive therapy this afternoon without any pharmacological trial first, with no informed consent or capacity assessment process performed before treatment in this patient
options.rationale: ECT is highly effective for catatonia but follows a lorazepam challenge in most pathways and requires informed consent or substitute decision-maker authorisation. (Source: RANZCP catatonia clinical resource; Australian ECT guidelines)

options.text: Initiate sertraline 50 mg daily as the sole intervention with the expectation of full response within 72 hours and no other pharmacological or psychiatric measures arranged at this admission
options.rationale: SSRI initiation does not address acute catatonia and may delay effective treatment. (Source: RANZCP catatonia clinical resource)

options.text: Commence haloperidol 5 mg intramuscular every 6 hours as monotherapy for catatonia, with no benzodiazepine challenge and no other pharmacological measure considered at this point in admission
options.rationale: Antipsychotics, especially typical agents, can precipitate neuroleptic malignant syndrome in catatonic patients; they are reserved for psychotic catatonia under specialist guidance after a benzodiazepine trial. (Source: RANZCP catatonia clinical resource)

options.text: Commence a single dose of biperiden 5 mg intramuscularly as the sole pharmacological intervention for catatonia and reassess in 24 hours with no other immediate measures
options.rationale: Anticholinergics have no established role in catatonia. (Source: RANZCP catatonia clinical resource)

options.text: Intravenous lorazepam 1 to 2 mg challenge with reassessment of catatonia score at 5 to 10 minutes; if responsive, scheduled lorazepam and plan for ECT if symptoms persist or recur
options.rationale: A lorazepam challenge (1 to 2 mg IV with reassessment within 10 minutes) confirms benzodiazepine-responsive catatonia in 60 to 80 percent of cases; ECT follows for non-responders. (Source: RANZCP catatonia clinical resource)

explanation.summary: Catatonia presents with mutism, immobility, posturing, waxy flexibility, negativism and stupor; the Bush-Francis scale aids diagnosis. After exclusion of medical mimics (infection, NMS, encephalitis, hypoactive delirium, drug effects), the first-line treatment is a lorazepam challenge (1 to 2 mg IV with reassessment within 10 minutes), followed by scheduled lorazepam typically 6 to 24 mg per day. ECT is the most effective second-line treatment when lorazepam fails or for malignant catatonia. Antipsychotics are deferred unless psychotic features require treatment with concurrent benzodiazepines.
key_points: Bush-Francis catatonia rating helps quantify response to treatment
Lorazepam challenge 1 to 2 mg IV reverses catatonia in 60 to 80 percent within minutes
Antipsychotic monotherapy risks neuroleptic malignant syndrome in catatonic patients
explanation.pearls: A 1 to 2 mg IV lorazepam challenge with reassessment at 5 to 10 minutes confirms benzodiazepine-responsive catatonia in around 70 percent and titrates to scheduled lorazepam 6 to 24 mg per day.
